original_thesis: The execution-quality stack. Checklists catch omissions; decomposition manages scope; premortem anticipates failure; prioritization allocates attention; mental simulation rehearses. Prevents competent people from making avoidable mistakes.
corrected_thesis: The execution-quality stack. Decomposition breaks work into manageable steps; checklists catch omissions in those steps; cognitive-load theory ensures the workload is within human capacity; delays warns about timing gaps that cause drift; feedback-loops signals whether execution is working; obligations-controls-mapping formalizes who owns what and what safeguards exist. This stack prevents competent people from making avoidable mistakes through rigorous process design.
what_this_stack_defeats: Execution errors, overlooked steps, unclear ownership, delayed feedback on performance
notes: Original thesis mentioned premortem, prioritization, mental-simulation — not members. Actual members form a stronger 'process engineering' stack than the original 'anticipate failure' framing. Obligations-controls-mapping gives it a compliance/governance angle.
